blocks.content: Paimon's demon realm had expanded its reach beyond the Central Continent, dominating the Southern Continent.

blocks.speaker: Clovis
blocks.content: Guh… Ugh!
A hulking man lay sprawled on the grand staircase of a massive mansion, groaning in agony.
Clovis, a magnate of the Kingdom of Kale.
Ranked second in the kingdom's wealth hierarchy and among the top 100 richest individuals across the Southern Continent, he was a man of immense influence.

blocks.speaker: Clovis
blocks.content: Why, why…
Clovis's face twisted with anguish.

blocks.speaker: Clovis
blocks.content: Why does it keep hurting!
First, he killed his maid.
Then his friend.
Then his 13 wives.
Then his 7 children.
All of it happened within a single day, and just moments ago, he had gutted Cassie—the being he loved most.

blocks.speaker: Clovis
blocks.content: Could she still be alive?
Clinging to a shred of sanity through the unbearable pain, Clovis hobbled down the staircase.
His wives and children, their eyes wide open, lay lifeless on the cold floor, staring at him.

blocks.speaker: Clovis
blocks.content: Ugh… It hurts. It hurts so much.
The pain was beyond human endurance.

blocks.speaker: Clovis
blocks.content: Cassie, are you still alive?
A large dog, one of a giant breed, lay sprawled with its guts spilled, its tongue hanging limply from its mouth.

blocks.speaker: Clovis
blocks.content: Just die. Just die already. I'm so tired.
Every stab with his longsword sent a jolt of pain through his body, as if his bones were shattering.

blocks.speaker: Clovis
blocks.content: Aaaah! Aaaaagh!
It wasn't until he could stab no more that Clovis finally confronted the grim reality of the lifeless dog before him.

blocks.speaker: Clovis
blocks.content: What the hell do you want from me? I've killed them all! What else do I have to give up to stop this pain?!
In a fit of rage, he hurled his longsword.
It ricocheted off the wall and struck the shelf holding his priceless porcelain.

blocks.speaker: Clovis
blocks.content: Gah!
His face turned pale as the porcelain wobbled before shattering on the floor.

blocks.speaker: Clovis
blocks.content: No! No!
Panicking, he rushed over and picked up a fragment from among the hundreds of pieces, his hands trembling.

blocks.speaker: Clovis
blocks.content: Damn it! Do you know how much this costs, you idiot?!
At that moment, Clovis realized his sense of touch had returned to normal.

blocks.speaker: Clovis
blocks.content: Money…
Seeing a glimmer of hope, he shook his head vigorously.

blocks.speaker: Clovis
blocks.content: No. How could I give up the money I've worked so hard for?
Since the age of seven, he hadn't taken a single day off. He wouldn't let anyone take his wealth.

blocks.speaker: Clovis
blocks.content: Bastards! Scum! You lowly creatures! Do you think I'd live like one of you?!
Yet even the worth of a porcelain shard was nothing but dust.

blocks.speaker: Clovis
blocks.content: No, no…
Terrified, Clovis trembled uncontrollably.

blocks.speaker: Clovis
blocks.content: Please, someone, make it stop. I'll pay 100 million gold—no, 1 billion gold! Just fix me!
Please… Please…

blocks.speaker: Clovis
blocks.content: Arrrgh!
As his threshold increased, the pain doubled in intensity, consuming his entire body.

blocks.speaker: Clovis
blocks.content: Help me! Somebody, help me!
Sixteen hours later.
By the time the World Health Organization arrived, the burning mansion illuminated the dawn sky.

blocks.speaker: Seriel
blocks.content: Phew.
Seriel closed the file for Case No. 13-465 and looked at Clovis's charred, hardened corpse lying on the bed.
It was like blackened coal.

blocks.speaker: Seriel
blocks.content: Immediate incineration?
After signing the document prepared by the mental mage, Seriel stood as the examiner covered the body with a sheet.

blocks.speaker: Seriel
blocks.content: Yes. We contacted everyone, but no one was willing to hold a funeral. Understandable, really.
It was a lonely death.

blocks.speaker: Seriel
blocks.content: All right. Take care of it.
Seriel's expression remained composed as she left the morgue and returned to her lab.
She was greeted by a wall covered in sticky notes and a large graph tracking time and data.
Reality set in.

blocks.speaker: Seriel
blocks.content: Hah…
The lives of the victims had long since been buried under the hundreds of reports she received daily.

blocks.speaker: Seriel
blocks.content: Why can't it be cured?
Her helplessness surged, and in an effort to deny it, she desperately summoned her rage.

blocks.speaker: Seriel
blocks.content: Argh! This is so frustrating!
She hurled anything within reach, smashing it against the walls.
Calm down! You have to stay calm!
Even though she knew that rage wouldn't solve the problem, her body refused to obey her mind.
Suddenly, the door to the lab swung open with a loud bang.

blocks.speaker: Shirone
blocks.content: Seriel! What's wrong?
Shirone stood there, alarmed.
When Seriel came to her senses, the room was in complete disarray, and blood trickled from a cut on her palm.

blocks.speaker: Seriel
blocks.content: Shirone…
Shirone saw the despair in her eyes and realized that the wound wasn't the real issue.

blocks.speaker: Shirone
blocks.content: Seriel, it's tough, but…
Before he could finish, Seriel buried her face into Shirone's chest, clutching his collar tightly as she sobbed.
Aah… Waaah…
Her cries were heart-wrenching.

blocks.speaker: Shirone
blocks.content: It's okay.
Shirone gently stroked her back, repeating the only words he could offer.

blocks.speaker: Shirone
blocks.content: It's okay. Everything will be okay.
How much time had passed?
By the time her tears soaked his clothes, Seriel's breathing had calmed.

blocks.speaker: Seriel
blocks.content: ... I'm sorry.
She held onto his collar tighter, as if she couldn't bring herself to face him.

blocks.speaker: Shirone
blocks.content: You don't need to apologize. It's okay to cry when things get hard. No one doubts how hard you're trying.
Maybe not everyone, but Shirone believed it.

blocks.speaker: Seriel
blocks.content: If you tell Amy, I'll kill you. Don't say a word until I do.
Haha! Got it. Now let's take care of that hand.
Though Seriel specialized in healing magic, emotional wounds required more than just skill.
The cut was long but thankfully not deep.

blocks.speaker: Seriel
blocks.content: What got you so worked up?
Sitting in a chair while Shirone treated her, Seriel lit a cigarette.

blocks.speaker: Seriel
blocks.content: I don't know. I've been having nightmares lately. Maybe I should cut back on case reviews.
Shirone gave her a wry smile.

blocks.speaker: Shirone
blocks.content: Yeah. It's tough.

blocks.speaker: Seriel
blocks.content: Emotion Disease… Having to look into the deepest parts of a person's soul—it's enough to drive anyone mad.
Seriel turned her head.

blocks.speaker: Shirone
blocks.content: You're incredible. How do you handle it? If I were in your shoes, I'd have gone crazy by now.

blocks.speaker: Seriel
blocks.content: You're doing great too. You've analyzed countless cases and developed guidelines to save people. Just remember, you've saved far more than you've lost.
Yeah…

blocks.speaker: Seriel
blocks.content: Seriel was grateful.

blocks.speaker: Seriel
blocks.content: I called you here because I have a favor to ask. Are you up for a trip?
A trip? For what?

blocks.speaker: Seriel
blocks.content: Seriel extinguished her cigarette and began rummaging through her desk.
Where is it…?
She eventually sat on the floor, sifting through scattered documents.

blocks.speaker: Seriel
blocks.content: Ah, here it is. Take a look.
Shirone glanced at the paper and saw a map marking the areas where Emotion Disease cases were reported.

blocks.speaker: Shirone
blocks.content: I see.
It was clearly an abnormal distribution.

blocks.speaker: Seriel
blocks.content: The disease spreads through the air, like a windborne contagion. While humans are a transmission vector, most nations have implemented quarantine policies, so the spread isn't as rampant. Therefore…

blocks.speaker: Shirone
blocks.content: The highest frequency of reported cases should be in the Central Continent, gradually decreasing toward the southwest. But this map shows patches of areas with no cases, almost as if the wind had stopped.

blocks.speaker: Seriel
blocks.content: Exactly. It's like there's a windless zone in the Southern Continent.
At first, I thought it might be due to a demonic invasion, but the Holy War confirmed that hell's armies are blocked at Kale's borders.

blocks.speaker: Seriel
blocks.content: So, I investigated whether these areas were administrative cities or military hubs.

blocks.speaker: Shirone
blocks.content: If they were, publicizing casualties could harm national interests.

blocks.speaker: Seriel
blocks.content: But they're not.
Seriel pointed at the map.

blocks.speaker: Seriel
blocks.content: My research showed no reason for these areas to withhold reports. In fact, most of them are just small, unremarkable fiefdoms.

blocks.speaker: Shirone
blocks.content: Did you send investigators?

blocks.speaker: Seriel
blocks.content: I reported it to the higher-ups. If my theory is convincing enough, they might issue an emergency directive, but prioritizing this over appeasing the nobility is uncertain.

blocks.speaker: Shirone
blocks.content: Waiting for approval is frustrating, so I kept digging. Then I had a thought: what if it's not that the cases aren't reported, but rather that these regions are somehow overcoming the disease?
Silence lingered.

blocks.speaker: Shirone
blocks.content: Hmm. Even if that's true, the simultaneous occurrence across the entire Southern Continent seems too large in scale. And it's happening in small villages, not major cities…
Shirone trailed off as he connected the blank areas on the map with an imaginary line.

blocks.speaker: Shirone
blocks.content: A route of some sort.

blocks.speaker: Seriel
blocks.content: That's my thought. So, I pulled an all-nighter investigating. I checked regional specialties, even farming tools, but nothing fit. I hit a wall until last night when I decided to take a different approach. If there's a product being distributed from one region across the Southern Continent, the biggest beneficiary would be its place of origin.
There was only one way to find the source.

blocks.speaker: Seriel
blocks.content: I went through every report, from No. 1 onward, marking each one. About two hours in, I realized I didn't need to. A specific place stood out for having no cases at all.
Shirone leaned forward.

blocks.speaker: Seriel
blocks.content: So I dropped the map and kept digging through the files until I found it. A region—or rather, a kingdom—that hasn't reported a single case of Emotion Disease.
Seriel drew a border around the southernmost part of the continent, above the Iron Kingdom.

blocks.speaker: Shirone
blocks.content: Know where this is?
Shirone's blood ran cold.

blocks.speaker: Seriel
blocks.content: The Kingdom of Kessia.

blocks.speaker: Shirone
blocks.content: Where Fermi was.

blocks.speaker: Seriel
blocks.content: A drug route.
Yes. It's a drug smuggling route. The drugs are produced in Kessia and spread across the Southern Continent. And wherever these drugs circulate, there are no reported cases of Emotion Disease.

blocks.speaker: Shirone
blocks.content: But… is that even possible?

blocks.speaker: Seriel
blocks.content: That's the problem. We've tested every type of morphine, sedative, tranquilizer, even experimental drugs, but nothing alleviates the symptoms of Emotion Disease. So what is this? What kind of substance could counteract the disease?

blocks.speaker: Shirone
blocks.content: 'It's too suspicious to be a coincidence. Besides, Fermi became a drug dealer long before the demonic realm opened. There's no way he could have predicted this…'
Without realizing it, Shirone muttered aloud.

blocks.speaker: Shirone
blocks.content: Ah.

blocks.speaker: Seriel
blocks.content: Maybe…

blocks.speaker: Shirone
blocks.content: 'Because it's Fermi.'
Shirone shook his head.

blocks.speaker: Shirone
blocks.content: I honestly don't know. But it's even harder to believe that he just got lucky. If all of this was calculated from the beginning…

blocks.speaker: Seriel
blocks.content: On that day, the one missing from his memory—what exactly had Fermi proposed to him?

blocks.speaker: Shirone
blocks.content: 'And why… Why did I accept his proposal?'

blocks.speaker: Seriel
blocks.content: Go to Kessia.
Seriel, who had been watching Shirone, pressed the ends of her pen together and spoke.

blocks.speaker: Seriel
blocks.content: No matter how I look at it, this isn't something an investigator can handle. Fermi has already taken control of Kessia, so launching an official investigation is tricky. I know it's a difficult request, but I'd appreciate it if you could go and confirm things yourself.

blocks.speaker: Shirone
blocks.content: Got it.

blocks.speaker: Seriel
blocks.content: Finding a way to suppress the Emotion Disease was important, but more than that, Fermi was someone Shirone had to meet before the holy war began.

blocks.speaker: Shirone
blocks.content: I'll leave now.
And so, Shirone set off for Kessia once again.
